questions.question_text: How long must a Mississippi broker retain transaction records?
questions.explanation: Mississippi requires brokers to retain all transaction records for a minimum of 3 years. This includes contracts, listing agreements, closing statements, correspondence, and trust account records.

questions.question_text: Which of the following must a Mississippi broker retain for 3 years?
questions.explanation: The 3-year retention requirement applies to ALL transaction records including contracts, listing agreements, closing statements, correspondence, and trust account records.

questions.question_text: A former client sues a broker over a transaction from 2 years ago. When may the broker destroy the related records?
distractors: Records cannot be destroyed during the suit
questions.explanation: If litigation is pending, records must be retained until the matter is concluded, even if the standard 3-year retention period has passed.

questions.question_text: A broker's trust account records from a transaction completed 2 years ago are requested by MREC. Must the broker produce them?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, they are within the retention period
distractors: No, trust records only need 1 year retention
Only if MREC obtains a court order
Only the bank provides trust records
questions.explanation: Trust account records are among the required documents subject to the 3-year retention rule. Records from 2 years ago are well within the retention period and must be produced for MREC inspection.

questions.question_text: Correspondence related to a transaction closed 4 years ago has no pending litigation. Must the broker still retain it?
distractors: Yes, correspondence must be kept 5 years
Only if the client requests retention
questions.explanation: Without pending litigation, the 3-year retention period applies. Since 4 years have passed with no active legal proceedings, the broker is no longer required to retain the correspondence.

questions.question_text: A Mississippi brokerage is closing permanently. What must happen to the transaction records?
questions.explanation: When a brokerage closes, records must be properly transferred to another party or placed in secure storage. The records cannot simply be abandoned or destroyed.

questions.question_text: After closing his brokerage, a broker transfers all records to a storage facility. Who remains responsible for the records?
questions.explanation: The broker remains personally responsible for records during the entire retention period, even after closing the business and transferring records to storage.

questions.question_text: A retiring broker's partner agrees to take custody of all firm records. Is this arrangement permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, records may be transferred properly
distractors: No, only MREC can accept records
Only if a court approves the transfer
No, records must stay with the original broker
questions.explanation: Records may be properly transferred to another broker or party when a brokerage closes. The key requirement is that the records remain accessible and secure during the retention period.

questions.question_text: MREC requests records from a brokerage that closed 18 months ago. The former broker says he discarded them at closing. What is the consequence?
distractors: No violation if the firm is dissolved
MREC must request from the Secretary of State
No consequence after a firm closes
questions.explanation: The broker remains responsible for records during the retention period regardless of business closure. Discarding records within the 3-year period violates Mississippi retention requirements.

questions.question_text: A broker is merging her firm with another brokerage. What happens to existing transaction records?
questions.correct_answer: They transfer to the surviving brokerage
distractors: They must be sent to MREC for safekeeping
They are destroyed at the merger date
Each agent takes their own records
questions.explanation: In a merger, existing records are properly transferred to the surviving brokerage entity. The new firm assumes the retention obligation for the remaining retention period.

questions.question_text: Are electronic transaction records legally acceptable in Mississippi real estate?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, with the same standing as paper
questions.explanation: Electronic records are fully acceptable in Mississippi and carry the same legal standing as paper records. They must be accessible and available for MREC inspection.

questions.question_text: MREC arrives for an audit and requests to view electronic records. What is the broker's obligation?
distractors: Print all records before the audit
questions.explanation: Electronic records must be accessible and available for MREC inspection. The broker must be able to produce them in a reviewable format when requested.

questions.question_text: A broker stores all transaction records in a cloud-based system. Is this method compliant with Mississippi law?
distractors: No, records must be stored locally
Only for records less than 1 year old
questions.explanation: Cloud storage is an acceptable form of electronic record keeping in Mississippi, provided the records remain accessible and can be produced for MREC inspection on request.

questions.question_text: A broker maintains some records electronically and others on paper. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a hybrid approach is acceptable
distractors: No, all records must be the same format
Only if MREC approves the system
Only for the first year of operation
questions.explanation: Mississippi does not require all records to be in a single format. A broker may maintain a mix of electronic and paper records as long as all records are accessible and meet the retention requirements.

questions.question_text: A broker's electronic records system crashes and data is lost. Has the broker violated retention requirements?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if records cannot be reproduced
distractors: Only if MREC requested records first
questions.explanation: The broker is responsible for maintaining accessible records during the retention period. A system crash resulting in permanent data loss means the broker has failed to meet the retention obligation, highlighting the importance of backups.
